content.text: Prompt and configuration versioning applies the same discipline to AI agent prompts and model settings that software engineering applies to source code: every change is tracked, every version is immutable, and any version can be restored instantly. Without it, a team that edits a production prompt in a shared dashboard has no record of what changed, no way to reproduce yesterday's behavior, and no safe path back when quality drops.

Prompt versioning is the practice of treating every revision of a system prompt, user-turn template, or chain-of-thought prefix as an immutable, uniquely identified artifact — exactly as Git treats a source file commit. Config versioning extends this to the parameters that accompany each call: model name, temperature, top-p, max tokens, stop sequences, tool schemas, and any other inference-time setting. Together they form a
that fully determines how an agent will behave for a given request.
A version is typically identified by a semantic tag (e.g.,

) and a content hash — the SHA-256 of the concatenated prompt text. The tag is human-readable and stable; the hash is a cryptographic guarantee that no one has silently edited the stored text. Agents resolve a version tag at startup and cache the bundle locally; they never read from a mutable "current" pointer in the middle of a request.
The practice covers three artifact classes.
set the agent's persona, capabilities, and constraints.
are parameterized strings injected with runtime data (user query, retrieved context, tool results).
hold inference parameters and model identifiers. All three can be versioned independently or locked together as a named release.

Prompt engineering feels lightweight until an agent reaches production traffic. At that point a single careless edit can silently degrade output quality for every user — and without versioning, the team may not even realize which change caused the regression or when. The three most damaging patterns that prompt versioning prevents are:

Developers who can edit a prompt in a web dashboard often do so under pressure, leave no audit trail, and produce a state that exists nowhere in source control. Six months later, nobody remembers why the prompt was worded that way.

Evaluation results from a Thursday experiment are meaningless if you cannot re-run them against exactly the same prompt text and model version three weeks later. Bug reproductions require knowing the exact version bundle that was active.

When a new prompt underperforms on live traffic, the only safe recovery is restoring a known-good version within minutes. Without a registry, teams resort to rewriting the prompt from memory or trawling Slack logs — a process that takes hours and often produces a subtly different prompt.

Prompt versioning also makes agent behavior auditable. Compliance teams and security reviewers need to know exactly which prompt was active during any incident. A registry with immutable entries and cryptographic hashes provides that evidence.
Restoring a previous prompt is a tag update in the registry — no redeploy, no code change. Recovery time drops from hours to seconds.
Every eval run records the prompt version it tested. Results are comparable across time and team members.
New prompt versions can be deployed to a canary cohort while the stable version serves the majority. Feature flags route individual users to specific versions.
Every version carries author, timestamp, and eval scores. Compliance queries answer themselves from the registry log.
Multiple engineers can work on competing prompt revisions in branches, run evals in CI, and merge the winner — the same workflow used for code.
When upgrading from one model version to another, the config bundle is updated as a new version. The old config remains available if the migration needs to be reversed.

A prompt registry is another service to deploy, monitor, and back up. For small teams with one or two agents, the operational cost may outweigh the benefit; a Git-tracked file and manual deploy works fine until scale demands more.

Every prompt change must go through a pull request, CI eval run, and promotion pipeline. This is the right trade-off for production, but it can feel burdensome during early exploratory phases where dozens of prompt variants need testing daily.
If an application hard-codes a version tag (e.g.,
), promoting a new version requires updating all callers. A level of indirection (a
alias that points at the current production version) reduces this but adds pointer-management complexity.

A CI gate that runs weak evals can still promote a bad prompt. The entire value of the pipeline depends on having a representative golden dataset and a reliable judge. Investing in versioning without investing in evals provides false confidence.

Versioning your prompt does not protect you from a model provider silently updating the underlying model weights. A prompt that scored 0.92 on eval in January may score 0.85 in March with the same version tag because the model changed. Periodic re-eval of stable versions catches this.
The core mechanism has four steps: authoring, validation, storage, and resolution. Each step is designed to be deterministic and auditable.
happens in a plain text or YAML file committed to Git. A typical repository layout keeps system prompts under
, with each change submitted as a pull request. The PR description records the motivation and links to eval results.

runs in CI. At minimum this checks that the prompt file parses, that required template variables are present, and that the model name is in the allowed list. Serious pipelines add an eval suite: the CI job sends the new prompt against a golden dataset of (input, expected_output) pairs, scores each with an LLM judge or a string-matching rule, and fails the PR if the aggregate score falls below the baseline established by the current production version. The regression threshold is typically expressed as an absolute score floor (e.g.,
in the prompt registry is immutable: a version, once published, is never overwritten. The registry computes

and stores it alongside the text. Any subsequent read that produces a different hash triggers an integrity alert. Version tags follow semver or a simple monotonic counter; the registry also maintains named aliases like
that point to a specific version.
is how a running agent obtains its prompt. Two strategies exist.
: the agent reads its version tag from an environment variable, fetches the bundle from the registry at startup, validates the hash, and caches the bundle in memory for the lifetime of the process.

pointer on each request, enabling hot-swap without restart. Per-request fetch adds a round-trip but allows zero-downtime promotions; a local TTL cache (e.g., 30 seconds) balances freshness against latency.

attrs.alt: Prompt and Config Versioning Pipeline: Git repo to CI (schema validation, eval suite, regression check, hash) to Prompt Registry (versioned entries with hash and metadata) to Staging (shadow eval, canary) to Production (hot swap). Rollback path returns from Production to Registry. Feature flags route user groups to specific versions.

content.text: The pipeline has five logical stages. Changes are authored in Git and trigger CI on pull request. CI validates, runs evals, and computes a content hash before publishing a versioned artifact to the prompt registry. The registry stores all versions as immutable records. New versions are first deployed to staging for shadow evaluation and canary traffic. After approval, the
alias is updated and agents hot-swap to the new bundle without restart. A rollback simply re-points
at the previous entry. Feature flags let specific user groups receive different version tags simultaneously.

Prompt versioning becomes essential once any of these conditions hold: the agent serves production traffic, more than one engineer works on prompts, the team runs A/B tests on prompt variants, or the product has compliance or audit requirements. A solo prototype that runs in a Jupyter notebook does not need a registry — a Git history of the prompt file is sufficient.
the agent serves real users, even in beta. A production regression caused by an untracked prompt edit is difficult to diagnose and damages trust.
multiple model versions or providers are in active use and each requires distinct prompts.
regulatory or contractual requirements mandate that the exact agent configuration at any past time can be produced on demand.
Skip the registry and use Git-only when
the project is exploratory, the team is one person, and there is no live traffic. The overhead of a registry is not justified.
Skip hot-swap and use startup-fetch when
the agent is stateless and restarts are cheap (e.g., serverless). Hot-swap adds complexity that is not needed if rollout can happen via a redeploy.
The following steps build a complete prompt versioning pipeline from scratch, starting with the file layout and ending with a running registry server.
directory for prompt text files and a
directory for YAML config bundles. Pair each prompt file with a matching config file using the same base name (e.g.,
Write a schema for the config YAML.
Use JSON Schema or Pydantic to enforce required fields:
. Validation runs as the first CI step and fails fast on malformed config.

Write a script that loads the golden dataset, sends each case through the new prompt + config, and scores the responses. The job fails if the aggregate score is below the stored baseline. Commit the baseline score alongside the prompt file.
On merge to main, a CI job computes
, assigns the next version number, and POSTs the bundle to the registry API. The registry stores the entry and returns an immutable version tag.
alias to point at the new version. The staging environment routes 5-10% of real traffic to agents that resolve this alias. Monitor quality metrics and latency for 24 hours.
After passing canary evaluation, update the
alias. Production agents that use per-request fetch pick up the new bundle within their TTL window. Record the previous stable version in a "last known good" field for instant rollback.
Log the resolved prompt version tag on every LLM call. Downstream analytics can then filter quality metrics by version, making regressions visible in dashboards.
A file-backed store suitable for a single-service setup. Prompts are stored as plain text files versioned by Git. The store reads from disk at startup, computes the hash, and caches the result.

import hashlib
import yaml
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass
from functools import lru_cache


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PromptBundle:
    """Immutable bundle of prompt text + inference config."""
    prompt_text: str
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    temperature: float
    content_hash: str  # sha256 of prompt_text


@lru_cache(maxsize=32)
def load_bundle(name: str, base_dir: str = "prompts") -> PromptBundle:
    """
    Load a prompt bundle from disk.
    Cached so the file is read once per process.
    """
    root = Path(base_dir)
    prompt_text = (root / f"{name}.txt").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    config = yaml.safe_load((root / f"{name}.yaml").read_text())

    content_hash = hashlib.sha256(prompt_text.encode()).hexdigest()

    return PromptBundle(
        prompt_text=prompt_text,
        model=config["model"],
        max_tokens=config["max_tokens"],
        temperature=config["temperature"],
        content_hash=content_hash,
    )


# Usage
bundle = load_bundle("summarize")
print(f"Loaded prompt hash: {bundle.content_hash[:12]}")
print(f"Model: {bundle.model}, max_tokens: {bundle.max_tokens}")
The config YAML alongside the prompt text file:

# prompts/summarize.yaml
model: claude-sonnet-4-5
max_tokens: 1024
temperature: 0.3
stop_sequences: []
version_label: "v2"       # human tag, not enforced
eval_baseline_score: 0.91  # fail CI if new score < this

A production agent that reads its prompt version tag from the environment, fetches the bundle from an HTTP registry, verifies the content hash, and falls back to the embedded default if the registry is unavailable.

import hashlib
import os
import time
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

REGISTRY_URL = os.environ.get("PROMPT_REGISTRY_URL", "http://prompt-registry:8080")
PROMPT_VERSION = os.environ.get("PROMPT_VERSION", "stable")  # or "summarize@v3"
CACHE_TTL_SECONDS = 30


@dataclass
class PromptBundle:
    prompt_text: str
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    temperature: float
    version_tag: str
    content_hash: str


class PromptClient:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._cache: Optional[PromptBundle] = None
        self._cache_at: float = 0.0

    def get_bundle(self, version: str = PROMPT_VERSION) -> PromptBundle:
        """
        Fetch from registry with a 30-second TTL cache.
        Falls back to the embedded default on registry failure.
        """
        now = time.monotonic()
        if self._cache and (now - self._cache_at) < CACHE_TTL_SECONDS:
            return self._cache

        try:
            resp = httpx.get(
                f"{REGISTRY_URL}/v1/bundles/{version}",
                timeout=2.0,
            )
            resp.raise_for_status()
            data = resp.json()

            # Verify integrity
            computed = hashlib.sha256(data["prompt_text"].encode()).hexdigest()
            if computed != data["content_hash"]:
                raise ValueError(
                    f"Hash mismatch for {version}: "
                    f"expected {data['content_hash'][:8]}, got {computed[:8]}"
                )

            bundle = PromptBundle(**data)
            self._cache = bundle
            self._cache_at = now
            return bundle

        except Exception as exc:
            if self._cache:
                # Serve stale cache rather than fail
                return self._cache
            raise RuntimeError(f"Registry unavailable and no cache: {exc}") from exc


# Module-level singleton
_client = PromptClient()


def call_llm(user_input: str) -> str:
    import anthropic

    bundle = _client.get_bundle()
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    response = client.messages.create(
        model=bundle.model,
        max_tokens=bundle.max_tokens,
        temperature=bundle.temperature,
        system=bundle.prompt_text,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_input}],
    )

    # Log version for traceability
    print(f"[agent] prompt_version={bundle.version_tag} hash={bundle.content_hash[:8]}")
    return response.content[0].text

"""ci_eval.py — run as part of CI to gate prompt promotions."""
import json
import sys
import yaml
import anthropic
from pathlib import Path

PROMPT_NAME = sys.argv[1]  # e.g. "summarize"
ROOT = Path(__file__).parent.parent


def load_bundle(name: str) -> dict:
    text = (ROOT / "prompts" / f"{name}.txt").read_text()
    config = yaml.safe_load((ROOT / "prompts" / f"{name}.yaml").read_text())
    return {"prompt_text": text, **config}


def load_golden(name: str) -> list[dict]:
    path = ROOT / "evals" / f"{name}.jsonl"
    return [json.loads(line) for line in path.read_text().splitlines() if line.strip()]


def score_response(response: str, expected: str) -> float:
    """Simple keyword overlap scorer; replace with LLM judge in production."""
    expected_words = set(expected.lower().split())
    response_words = set(response.lower().split())
    if not expected_words:
        return 1.0
    return len(expected_words & response_words) / len(expected_words)


def main():
    bundle = load_bundle(PROMPT_NAME)
    golden = load_golden(PROMPT_NAME)
    baseline = bundle.get("eval_baseline_score", 0.80)

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    scores = []

    for case in golden:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model=bundle["model"],
            max_tokens=bundle["max_tokens"],
            temperature=bundle["temperature"],
            system=bundle["prompt_text"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        score = score_response(resp.content[0].text, case["expected"])
        scores.append(score)
        print(f"  case={case['id']} score={score:.3f}")

    aggregate = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    print(f"\nAggregate score: {aggregate:.3f}  (baseline: {baseline})")

    if aggregate < baseline:
        print(f"FAIL: score {aggregate:.3f} < baseline {baseline}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print("PASS: eval gate cleared")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Consider a legal document processing service that uses three agents in a pipeline: a clause extractor, a risk scorer, and a summary writer. The product team wants to improve the summary writer's tone without affecting the extractor or scorer. With prompt versioning, only the summary writer's prompt is updated, tested in isolation, and promoted via canary while the other two agents continue running their current versions.
The registry stores each agent's current version in a namespaced key. The deployment pipeline updates the summary-writer's alias from

while the other two agents remain at their existing versions. If users complain that v5 summaries are too terse, rolling back is a single API call to re-point the stable alias, taking effect within 30 seconds on all instances.

"""registry_client.py — minimal example of a registry HTTP client."""
import httpx

REGISTRY = "http://prompt-registry:8080"


def publish_version(agent: str, prompt_text: str, config: dict) -> str:
    """Publish a new version; returns the assigned version tag."""
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"{REGISTRY}/v1/agents/{agent}/versions",
        json={"prompt_text": prompt_text, "config": config},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["version_tag"]   # e.g. "summary-writer@v5"


def promote(agent: str, version_tag: str, alias: str = "stable") -> None:
    """Move an alias (stable/canary) to a specific version."""
    resp = httpx.put(
        f"{REGISTRY}/v1/agents/{agent}/aliases/{alias}",
        json={"version_tag": version_tag},
        timeout=5,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()


def rollback(agent: str) -> str:
    """Restore the previous stable version; returns the tag that was restored."""
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"{REGISTRY}/v1/agents/{agent}/rollback",
        timeout=5,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["restored_tag"]


# --- Example CI/CD usage ---

NEW_PROMPT = """
You are a legal document summarizer. Write a clear, concise summary
of the provided document in 3-5 sentences. Use plain language.
Focus on obligations, deadlines, and risk areas.
""".strip()

CONFIG = {"model": "claude-sonnet-4-5", "max_tokens": 512, "temperature": 0.2}

# Publish and promote to canary for shadow testing
tag = publish_version("summary-writer", NEW_PROMPT, CONFIG)
promote("summary-writer", tag, alias="canary")
print(f"Canary: {tag} — monitor metrics for 24h")

# After passing canary: promote to stable
promote("summary-writer", tag, alias="stable")
print(f"Promoted to stable: {tag}")

# On regression: rollback in <1 minute
# restored = rollback("summary-writer")
# print(f"Rolled back to: {restored}")
Editing prompts in place in the registry.

The defining property of a versioned registry is that entries are immutable. If the registry API allows overwriting a version, the integrity guarantee disappears. Enforce append-only writes at the API level; reject any PUT to an existing version tag.
Fetching the bundle without re-computing and verifying the hash defeats tamper detection. Always verify
before using a prompt, even from a trusted internal registry.
Using a mutable 'latest' pointer in production.
Resolving "latest" on every call means agents silently pick up new prompts the moment they are published, with no canary phase. Name your aliases intentionally:
for shadow traffic, and pin dev environments to explicit version tags.
Not recording the version in logs.

If the LLM response log does not include the prompt version tag, diagnosing whether a quality issue is a prompt regression or a model drift issue requires guesswork. Log the version tag (and optionally the first 8 bytes of the content hash) on every inference call.

LLM outputs are stochastic. Running each eval case once gives a noisy score. Use temperature 0 for eval runs (if your model supports it) or average over 3-5 runs per case and report the mean. Store the per-case scores, not just the aggregate, to diagnose which inputs regressed.
A prompt text is only half the story. Changing
from 0.3 to 0.7 can alter agent behavior as much as rewriting the prompt. Version the full config bundle together with the prompt text.
Store prompts in Git. Require pull requests. Apply the same code review practices to prompt text as to business logic.
Pin versions explicitly in deployment manifests.
The Kubernetes deployment or Lambda environment that runs the agent should declare
explicitly. The stable alias is for gradual rollout; after promotion, update the manifest to pin the now-stable version tag and keep stable as the rollout mechanism for the next change.
Write eval cases that cover edge inputs, not just happy paths.
A golden dataset of ten easy inputs gives a false sense of safety. Include cases with adversarial inputs, empty inputs, very long inputs, and inputs in languages other than the primary target.

Each version entry in the registry should link to a CHANGELOG entry explaining why the prompt was changed, what eval delta was observed, and who approved it. This is invaluable during incident review.

Model providers update underlying weights without version-bumping the model name in some cases. A scheduled CI job that re-runs the eval suite against the current stable version detects model drift before users do.
Use semantic version numbers for breaking changes.
A major version bump (v2 → v3) signals to callers that the output format or contract changed, not just the wording. Minor bumps are wording tweaks that should not break downstream parsing.

The registry is a read-heavy, write-rarely service. Reads happen at agent startup or on each TTL expiry; writes happen only when a new version is published (typically once or twice a week for a mature product). This access pattern maps well to a simple key-value store or even a static file server.
For latency-sensitive deployments, serve the registry response from a CDN or object storage layer (S3, GCS) with an aggressive cache-control header (e.g.,

). Each version's JSON file is immutable and can be cached indefinitely at the CDN edge; only the alias pointer files (stable, canary) need short TTLs. With this setup, a fleet of 10,000 agent instances can all fetch their prompt bundle in under 5ms per call from edge cache, regardless of the number of registered versions.

The in-process TTL cache in the agent (30 seconds in the examples above) dramatically reduces registry traffic. A fleet of 500 agents, each processing 100 requests/minute, would generate 50,000 registry fetches per minute without caching. With a 30-second TTL, each agent fetches at most 2 times per minute, reducing registry load by 99%.

Eval CI jobs are the main computational cost. Running 200 golden cases through an LLM at $3/million output tokens with an average 200-token response costs approximately $0.12 per eval run. The cost scales with the golden dataset size and eval frequency, not the number of running agents. Parallelizing eval cases with
or a thread pool cuts wall-clock time from minutes to seconds.
Version history, A/B labels, per-version metric breakdown, open-source self-hostable
Tracks every LLM call with its prompt version; integrates with major providers
Side-by-side comparison of prompt variants; integrates with MLflow runs for combined model + prompt tracking
Links prompt versions to training runs and eval results in one lineage graph
Natively integrates with Bedrock inference; IAM-based access control per version
Zero additional dependencies; object storage provides immutable versioned keys via object versioning feature
LLM-as-judge eval pipelines with per-version score tracking; open-source
The prompt registry holds privileged content. A malicious actor who can write to the registry can change the behavior of every agent that trusts it. Harden the registry with the following controls:

Only the CI pipeline's deployment service account can call the publish and alias-update endpoints. Engineers have read access to audit versions but cannot write directly. Enforce this with RBAC, not just convention.

The CI pipeline signs the published bundle with a private key held in a secrets manager. Agents verify the signature before loading the bundle. This prevents a registry compromise from injecting unsigned prompts.
Prompts are not secrets, but they often reference placeholders that are filled from secrets at runtime (e.g.,
). Keep the registry read-world-accessible but fill sensitive values from a separate secrets store (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) at agent startup, never storing them in the prompt itself.

The agent must continue serving requests if the registry is temporarily down. Use a stale cache with a generous TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) before failing open. If the cache has expired and the registry is unreachable, fail closed with a 503 rather than serving an unverified or blank prompt.
Detect prompt injection in versioned templates.

If the prompt template includes user-controlled variables, run an injection scanner in the CI eval suite. A prompt that concatenates raw user input into the system prompt without sanitization is a high-severity vulnerability regardless of which version is deployed.

Do not purge old versions. Regulatory audits may require producing the exact prompt text active during a specific historical period. Storage cost for text files is negligible; the compliance value is high.
Every production prompt and its inference config should be stored as an immutable, content-hashed version in a registry, never edited in place.
CI must gate every prompt change with an eval suite; a regression threshold enforced in the pipeline is the primary defense against degraded agent quality.
Named aliases (stable, canary) decouple promotion from redeploy: updating an alias enables hot-swap on live agents within one TTL window.
Agents must log the prompt version tag on every LLM call; without this, diagnosing regressions in production is nearly impossible.
Registry reads should be protected by a short in-process TTL cache (20-60 seconds) to eliminate latency and reduce registry traffic by orders of magnitude.
Write access to the registry must be locked down to the CI service account; human-direct writes bypass the eval gate and invalidate the safety guarantee.
Schedule periodic re-evals of stable versions to detect model drift — a prompt that passed CI six months ago may perform differently today if the underlying model has been updated by the provider.

For early-stage projects, Git-tracked files with a manual deploy cycle are sufficient; invest in a full registry only when the team size, traffic, or compliance requirements justify the added operational complexity.
